physicalDetails.detail: Young human prince in polished plate that stays bright even in Strahnbrad's mud, golden hair damp at the temples, mouth hardened by command and lack of sleep, and Light's Vengeance resting across one forearm with practiced ease.
He looks older than Jaina remembers — still handsome, still easy in his own charisma, but narrowed by fatigue into something harder and less open than memory wants him to be.

Chapter 11 places him over a trestle table pinned with daggers and dispatch tubes, the old princely charisma cut harder by command until even Sylvanas reads him as a man shaped more by certainty than wisdom.

voiceMarkers.detail: Publicly courteous and almost effortlessly charming, opening with social ease even in a wet war camp.
Listens hard enough to make disbelief sound reasonable; his refusal arrives wrapped in patience, hierarchy, and a tone that keeps trying to sound fair while closing every door.
When challenged too directly, the courtesy shows its teeth — quiet, controlled, and more dangerous for never needing to raise his voice.
Chapter 11 gives him briefing-room velocity: sharp clean questions, fast pattern recognition, and orders that begin before the intelligence has fully stopped being spoken.

beliefs.belief: A visible civilian crisis in front of him deserves priority over a harder-to-prove invisible one moving through grain and rumor.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active and unchallenged inside the chapter; the prose treats it as understandable but dangerously incomplete.

beliefs.belief: Command cannot be rerouted on the strength of one argument without cleaner proof and clearer naming than Jaina can yet provide.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active at chapter end; he partially credits the warning but still refuses major action.

beliefs.belief: Panic can kill as efficiently as plague if a warning is spread carelessly through a still-working city.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed as part of his governing instinct, though the chapter frames waiting for permission as its own danger.

beliefs.belief: Once the grain pattern clicks, moving immediately is holier than waiting for the last missing layer of explanation.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed as his active chapter-11 stance and presented as both effective and dangerous.

beliefs.belief: Once Hearthglen starts failing again, speed toward the next city matters more than consolidating the line already under him.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active in chapter 14 and framed as both understandable and dangerous.

goalsAtChapterEnd.active: Drive the main body south-east toward Stratholme without ceding tempo.
goalsAtChapterEnd.underlying: Beat the plague by reaching the next fire before delay turns one failing city into two.

emotionalStateAtChapterEnd.state: Off-page but clearly overdriven — chapter 14 leaves him ahead of the survivors, ahead of consolidation, and already pulling the war toward its next irreversible decision.

decisionsThisChapter.detail: Granted Jaina and Sylvanas an audience inside his command space instead of dismissing them outright from the saddle.
Heard the plague case through to actionable requests, then refused to divert major force toward Andorhal while Blackrock raiders were still taking civilians.
Issued only a limited follow-up order to quartermasters about trouble from Andorhal's grain yards, making the warning secondary to the war already in front of him.
Accepts Sylvanas’s plague briefing as actionable truth, immediately ordering local granaries checked, suspect stores burned, and the quartermaster line sealed.
Launches the mounted response before the full analytical foundation is complete, turning the warning into cavalry tempo within the hour.
Invites Sylvanas to ride with him, then lets her refuse without contest once his own line of advance is set.
Does not remain at Hearthglen after the town nearly fails again after dark.
Takes the main body south-east at speed toward Stratholme.

abilitiesDemonstrated.ability: Turns a wet field camp into a personal command center by sheer presence; the space bends around him before he says a word.
Balances princely charm and battlefield authority, shifting between greeting, command, and warning without visible strain.
Holds to a chosen priority under emotional pressure, even when Jaina's knowledge and their shared history both press on the decision.
Converts a map full of partial plague data into immediate military action the moment the frame becomes legible to him.
Turns a large field camp inside out quickly, translating personal conviction into mounted mobilization with almost no lag.
Can turn a provisional hold into renewed offensive movement almost immediately, keeping an army in motion even while the field is still unstable behind him.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Uses 'Arthas' with no title, letting private history override protocol.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Sylvanas Windrunner
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Reduces him to 'the prince' once the exchange becomes a command problem instead of a personal one.

howHeAddressesOthers.pattern: Begins with 'Lady Proudmoore' in polished public courtesy, then uses 'Jaina' softly when trying to make refusal sound reasonable.
Uses the formal 'Ranger-General,' acknowledging rank without pretending intimacy.

bodyLanguagePatterns.detail: Makes a center of himself without obvious effort; command space organizes around his body before the dialogue does.
His shoulders tighten almost imperceptibly at the word plague, and he rests the hammer's head in the mud when choosing the stance that lets him refuse without retreating.
When he issues the small quartermaster concession, he does it without looking away from Jaina, as if even the gesture must remain under his control.

Chapter 11 makes his body language the story of acceleration: hand flattening over Hearthglen on the map, grip whitening on the table edge when challenged, and the whole tent changing temperature once he starts issuing orders.

knowledge.detail: Knows Strahnbrad is under active Blackrock pressure and that civilians are still being taken or threatened in the immediate theater around him.
Hears that the plague is moving through grain toward Andorhal and that Jaina and Sylvanas believe the road itself is part of the war.
Accepts that there may be trouble in Andorhal's grain system, but not at a level he considers sufficient for immediate large-scale redeployment.

By chapter 9 Arthas matters to the surviving field team less as the man who already refused once and more as the outside command lever still capable of moving faster than committee process if reached in time.

Learns the plague is moving through ordinary grain routes, that Hearthglen and likely Stratholme sit on the same compromised map, and that some of his own camp sickness may already belong to the same system.
Hears Uther’s warning in real time and ignores its braking function, choosing motion over reflection once the target locks.
Now has field confirmation from the relay line that Hearthglen nearly failed again after dark and that the wounded are being burned before kin finish the words over them.
By chapter 14 his movement has made Stratholme the live crisis point rather than a future destination.

arc.lie: A threat that cannot yet be struck cleanly can wait behind the one already burning in daylight.
arc.truth: The invisible war in the grain is already inside his visible one, and delay is its own catastrophic choice.
arc.currentPosition: Chapter 14 intensifies the chapter-11 pattern instead of correcting it. Arthas is still moving on the right threat, but he is doing it with the same catastrophic appetite for speed that keeps turning insight into momentum before anyone can price the cost.

arcHistory.currentPosition: Chapter 6 introduces Arthas as still heroic-looking, still publicly reasonable, and already trapped in the command habit that will make him late to the plague. The chapter does not villainize him into pettiness; it shows the fatal shape of his priorities.
Chapter 11 inverts the earlier refusal without healing it. Arthas now recognizes the right enemy quickly, but the same absolutist command instinct simply flips from delay into dangerous over-speed.

goalsHistory.active: Contain the Blackrock crisis around Strahnbrad while folding Andorhal grain trouble into the command queue rather than the center of operations.
goalsHistory.underlying: Hold military order around the threat he can strike today and avoid tearing apart his current response on evidence he judges incomplete.

goalsHistory.active: Drive the mounted column south toward Hearthglen and the infected grain line before the plague can outrun visible force again.
goalsHistory.underlying: Save Lordaeron by striking the threat at once, trusting decisiveness to compensate for what he still does not understand.

emotionalStateHistory.state: Tired, fixed, and armored behind courtesy — not ignorant of the warning, simply committed to a different priority and settled into that commitment with his full weight.
Charged by certainty and already half gone down the road before anyone has finished naming the cost of his certainty.
